title: Specifying Libraries and Functions
text: Specifying Libraries and Functions The [USER_3GL] section of the assignment file describes 3GL shared libraries and/or functions that will be called by your Uniface application. You can call 3GL code only via an entry in the [USER_3GL] section. (Prior to Uniface 8, information was known from the link information (Unix), or from the [DEMANDLOAD] section in the usys.ini file (for MS Windows). ) When you have multiple functions in your shared library that you want to call, you must enumerate them, separated by commas, in the [USER_3GL] assignment entry. For example: [USER_3GL] acttest(UPCASE, LOCASE, CAPITALIZE) Copy Note: On all platforms, the names of functions are case-sensitive. The name of the shared library is case-sensitive only on Unix and Linux, and case-insensitive on all other platforms. Limitations The length of an assignment line must not be longer than 511 characters (anything more will be truncated). Furthermore, you can specify only up to about 20 functions in one assignment. We recommend that you stay well below these limits, to avoid complications as well as to maintain readability. Lines that are too long or have too many functions should be broken up into multiple lines. The name of the shared library must be repeated on each line. For example: [USER_3GL] my3gllib(function1, function2, function3, function4, function5, function6) my3gllib(function7, function8, function9, function10) Copy This might get inconvenient quickly if your shared library contains more than a few dozen functions. The solution for this is to leave out the function list and the enclosing parentheses. Uniface will then inspect the shared library at start-up time, and find all the exported functions for you. The above assignments could thus be written as, [USER_3GL] my3gllib Copy This will obviously cause a certain delay at start up, which might be noticeable, depending on the size of the shared library and the number of functions in it. For best performance, we recommend specifying the functions as described above. Note the following restrictions when you decide to leave out the list of functions: This functionality is not implemented on the iSeries (AS/400 It is not possible to mix the two methods. You can specify either all the entry points, or none. Uniface will look up the functions only if it does not find a left parenthesis in the assignment line. On Unix and Linux, it is currently necessary to use the fully qualified path name of the shared library, or else the shared library must be located in the current working directory. This restriction might be lifted in a future patch. This auto-detect mechanism has nothing to do with the /preload switch that you can add to the shared library name. That switch can be used regardless of whether you specify the list of functions. Specifying /preload means only that the library will be dynamically loaded at start-up time, but it does not imply that Uniface will search for the functions. Using /preload increases start-up time, but avoids delays at run time caused by demand-loading of shared libraries. These are some examples of possible assignments: [USER_3GL] my3gllib/preload otherlib/preload (function1, function2) d:/temp/foobar.dll (foo, bar) c:/winnt/system32/user32.dll /preload Copy Note that the /preload switch always comes between the shared library name and the function list (if any). Using Signature Properties As an alternative to specifying the shared library and/or functions in the assignment file, you can define the name of your shared library in the signature itself. You do this in the Signature Editor, by clicking the More button next to the Implementations field, and then clicking the Properties button. On this form, you can enter the physical name of the shared library that contains the operations of this signature. You can use either a fully qualified name or a partially qualified name (without path and/or suffix), just as you can do in the assignment file. An example is Uniface’s internal use of selected Windows API functions. There is a signature named KERNEL32API that defines some functions in the Windows kernel32.dll . The signature definition specifies the DLL name kernel32.dll , so assignments are not necessary. The full path name is not necessary (which is a good thing, because the path can vary between different versions of Windows), because Windows locates its system DLLs at run time via the PATH variable. In fact, the path name and file suffix can be omitted for all platforms, making it possible to create a platform-neutral 3GL signature. Related Links Signatures A Uniface signature describes the remote interface of a component. The interface enables other components to address the component without knowing anything about the implementation. This allows components of different origin to communicate through their respective interfaces. Signature Editor The Signature Editor enables you to define, edit, and compile component signatures for both Uniface and non-Uniface components. Define a Component Signature Use the Signature Editor to create or modify component signatures. Signatures of Uniface components are automatically generated during compilation. Signatures of non-Uniface components can be created or imported. [USER_3GL] Use the [USER_3GL] section of the assignment file to lists third-party components and Uniface 3GL extensions to be made available to Uniface. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
